findings.message: `adr-editor.md` is added to `REQUIRED_AGENT_FILES`, but `.claude/agents/adr-editor.md` is not tracked in the diff/index (it only appears as an untracked working-tree file). On a clean checkout of the committed changes, `verify_agent_definitions` will require this file and fail `verify-orchestra-local` unless the agent file is added to the tracked changes or removed from the required list.

`usecase::render_track_views` would move Markdown/view rendering and file I/O into the usecase layer, which is the wrong boundary for this repository's hexagonal split. The validation step can be shared, but the render pipeline should stay infrastructure-owned; otherwise this TODO would invert the dependency direction instead of just extracting the stale-signal check.

The new parenthetical still says the workflow's actual Markdown generation and file write happen "via infra port", which is inconsistent with the pure-orchestrator boundary described here and with the existing `contract_map_workflow.rs` pattern. As written, it can mislead implementers into reintroducing the same layer-boundary leak this TODO is trying to remove.

`track/workflow.md` now says the default profile assigns `adr-editor` to Claude Code, but the sample profiles in this change only define `orchestrator`, `spec-designer`, `impl-planner`, `type-designer`, `implementer`, `reviewer`, and `researcher`. That leaves the workflow pointing at a capability users cannot select from the documented profiles, so the ADR back-and-forth path is unusable as written.

`adr-editor` is referenced by the new workflow, but this source-of-truth capability map never defines it. On a red Phase 1 signal the orchestrator will try to re-invoke `adr-editor`, and capability resolution will fail before the ADR edit loop can start. The same omission also exists in the three sample profile files.

Deleting the backing `track-plan` skill file leaves the repo with no `track-plan` skill entry while many existing docs still point at `.claude/skills/track-plan/SKILL.md`. That turns the documented skill path into a dead link and breaks any workflow that still relies on the skill loader instead of the new command docs.

The new phased workflow says `/track:plan` now orchestrates Phase 0-3 to produce `metadata.json`, `spec.json`, type catalogues, `impl-plan.json`, and `task-coverage.json`, but the same document still later tells users that `/track:plan` creates the old artifact set (`metadata.json`, `plan.md`, `spec.md`, `verification.md`) after approval and after clearing `track/tech-stack.md` TODOs. Those are incompatible contracts for the canonical planning command, so agents following this user-facing workflow can execute the obsolete artifact flow instead of the new phased one.

Deleting the backing `track-plan` skill file leaves the repo with no `track-plan` skill entry while live guidance still references `track-plan` as a skill workflow. That turns the documented skill path into a dead link and breaks any workflow that relies on skill discovery instead of only the command docs.

The new phased workflow says `/track:plan` orchestrates Phase 0-3 to produce `metadata.json`, `spec.json`, type catalogues, `impl-plan.json`, and `task-coverage.json`, but the same document still later tells users that `/track:plan` creates the old artifact set (`metadata.json`, `plan.md`, `spec.md`, `verification.md`) after approval and after clearing `track/tech-stack.md` TODOs. These are incompatible contracts for the canonical planning command, so agents can follow the obsolete artifact flow instead of the new phased one.
`adr-editor` is referenced in the new `/track:plan` back-and-forth flow, but `.harness/config/agent-profiles.json` never declares that capability, so the routing target cannot be resolved.

`/adr:add` still templates `## Status` with `Accepted | Proposed`, but `knowledge/conventions/pre-track-adr-authoring.md` explicitly forbids `Status`/`approved` fields. The command will generate ADRs that violate the convention it is meant to enforce.

`type-designer.md` still requires `approved: true` on every type entry, which keeps an approval state in the catalogue schema even though the new workflow convention removes ceremonial approval/status fields.

`/track:design` still lists only 12 `TypeDefinitionKind` variants and omits `secondary_adapter`, while `type-designer.md` now treats the taxonomy as 13 variants. The command and writer no longer agree on the accepted catalogue schema.

`/track:plan` now routes ADR back-and-forth to `capabilities.adr-editor`, but this repo's capability map does not define an `adr-editor` entry. As written, the new ADR escalation path cannot be resolved, so Phase 1 🔴 cannot actually re-invoke the writer it names.

`/adr:add` still requires a `## Status` section with `Accepted` / `Proposed`, but the new pre-track ADR convention explicitly forbids ADR status fields and the ceremony-minimization convention removes artificial `Status` / `approved` state. ADRs created by this command would violate the policy the rest of the refactor is introducing.

`/track:design` still hard-codes 12 `TypeDefinitionKind` variants and never mentions `secondary_adapter`, while the new `type-designer` agent treats the taxonomy as 13 variants. Phase 2 will not be able to describe or validate `secondary_adapter` entries, so the command and writer now disagree on the accepted catalogue schema.

`adr-editor` is now part of the orchestration flow, but the agent index never adds an `adr-editor` entry or file the way it does for `spec-designer`, `impl-planner`, and `type-designer`. That leaves the ADR repair loop in `track/plan.md` and `08-orchestration.md` without a custom prompt contract or model pin, so the new back-and-forth path is under-specified.

The language-convention paragraph still cites `## Status` as an example of a valid English section header, but this file and `pre-track-adr-authoring.md` now explicitly remove ADR status fields. That stale example conflicts with the new no-`Status` workflow and can cause freshly authored ADRs to reintroduce the forbidden section.

The new 13-kind list includes `secondary_adapter`, but the context step still tells readers to find the taxonomy by grepping `tddd-taxonomy`. That grep only surfaces the older 12-kind ADR, so the instructions for the new kind are never actually loaded.

This command now says new ADRs must omit `## Status`, but it still tells the agent to read `knowledge/adr/README.md` as the ADR template. The README template still requires `Status`, so a straightforward read/clone path produces ADRs that violate this command's own convention.

The new `secondary_adapter` kind is introduced earlier in the command, but the canonical declaration checklist still only enumerates `expected_variants`, `transitions_to`, and `expected_methods`, and it explicitly says struct-only variants carry no extra fields. That omits `implements`, so `/track:design` can produce incomplete `secondary_adapter` entries that fail downstream validation.

The new workflow policy says artificial approval state is removed, but `/track:design` still instructs the type-designer to emit `approved: true`, preserve existing `approved` status, and iterate until approved. That keeps generating the approval field this change says is no longer part of track artifacts, leaving the harness-policy docs internally inconsistent.

Phase 2 is now documented elsewhere as consuming the Phase 1 `spec.json`, and `type-designer.md` declares `track/items/<id>/spec.json` as an input, but `/track:design` still tells the command to read `spec.md` or fall back to `plan.md` instead. That fallback allows type catalogue authoring to proceed without the spec SSoT and can produce entries without reliable `SpecRef` coverage, contradicting the new SoT Chain.

The custom Opus-pinned agent list omits the new `.claude/agents/adr-editor.md` even though `agent-profiles.json`, `/track:plan`, and the agent README register `adr-editor` as a Claude subagent. This leaves the ADR back-and-forth capability out of the documented model override path and makes the capability mapping inconsistent.

Step 1 now prefers `spec.json`, but still instructs the agent to read a Markdown `## Related Conventions (Required Reading)` section from the selected artifact. `spec.json` stores this data as `related_conventions[]`, so the preferred path has no such section and can cause the type-design flow to miss required convention inputs unless it falls back to the rendered view despite declaring JSON authoritative. Specify `related_conventions[]` handling for `spec.json`, and use the Markdown heading only for `spec.md` / `plan.md`.

The design command still requires presenting the type design to the user for review before writing artifacts. That conflicts with the newly added ceremony-minimization policy and `/track:plan` gate policy, which say artifact generation should use post-hoc review and pre-approval is reserved for irreversible actions. Keeping this pre-write acceptance loop preserves the old approval ceremony for a reversible generated artifact.

`/track:design` now prefers `spec.json`, but this step still tells the agent to read conventions from a Markdown `## Related Conventions (Required Reading)` section. `spec.json` has a `related_conventions[]` field rather than Markdown headings, so the preferred path can fail to load required conventions whenever `spec.json` exists. The instruction should branch on artifact type and read `related_conventions[]` from JSON.

The default capability assignment omits `adr-editor` even though the new profile defines it as a Claude capability and the same rule file later includes it in provider resolution. This leaves the top-level routing summary inconsistent with the registry and the new back-and-forth ADR flow.

`/track:design` now prefers `spec.json`, but this step still instructs the command to read a Markdown `## Related Conventions (Required Reading)` section from the selected artifact. Existing `spec.json` stores this as the `related_conventions` field, so when the preferred JSON SSoT exists the command cannot follow its own convention-loading instruction and may skip required conventions.

The staged diff adds references to `adr-editor.md`, but `.claude/agents/adr-editor.md` is untracked and absent from `git diff --cached`. If the current patch is committed as staged, these references and the new `adr-editor` capability point at a missing subagent definition.

The user workflow still describes `/track:plan` as doing approval confirmation and creating track artifacts only after user approval, which contradicts the new ceremony-minimization convention and the rewritten `/track:plan` post-hoc artifact review flow. This leaves the public guide telling users to follow the removed approval ceremony.

This convention says `spec.json / 型カタログ / impl-plan` should all cite ADRs directly via `AdrRef`, but the new SoT Chain in `/track:plan` only allows the spec layer to reference ADRs and explicitly forbids `type catalogue → ADR` layer skips. If agents follow this, type catalogues or implementation plans can be authored with forbidden direct ADR references instead of routing through spec/type refs.

`/track:design` still instructs design-phase artifacts to cite ADRs via `AdrRef`. That conflicts with the Phase 2 contract described in `/track:plan`, where type catalogue entries are evaluated through `spec_refs[]` and direct ADR references are a layer skip.

The type-designer output contract allows catalogue `informal_grounds[]` to be promoted to `AdrRef`, which again permits type catalogue entries to bypass the spec layer. Under the new SoT Chain this should be limited to `SpecRef` / appropriate convention refs, with ADR rationale propagated through `spec_refs[]`.

This convention says `spec.json`, type catalogues, and `impl-plan` all cite ADRs directly through `AdrRef`. That conflicts with the SoT Chain in `/track:plan`, where Phase 2 catalogue entries reference the spec via `spec_refs[]` and `type catalogue → ADR` is explicitly a forbidden layer skip. Agents following this convention can author downstream artifacts with direct ADR refs that the Phase 2 contract does not allow.

`/track:design` repeats the forbidden direct ADR path by telling design-phase artifacts to cite an ADR via `SpecRef` / `AdrRef`. The rest of the new plan says the type-contract gate evaluates `spec_refs[]` only and forbids `type catalogue → ADR`, so this command can instruct the type-designer/orchestrator to produce invalid SoT-chain references.

The type-designer output contract allows catalogue `informal_grounds[]` to be promoted to `AdrRef`, which lets type catalogue entries bypass the spec layer. Under the new Phase 2 contract, catalogue entries should resolve informal grounds to `SpecRef` or leave them informal until the spec/ADR is updated upstream; otherwise the agent can emit references that `/track:plan` defines as layer skips.

The standard flow still starts users at `/track:plan <feature>` and describes the old pre-artifact approval flow, but the new strict workflow requires a pre-track ADR before `/track:plan` and uses post-hoc artifact review. Following this section skips the required ADR authoring/selection step and can hit the new `/track:plan` pre-flight stop.

`adr-editor` is added to the documented capability table, but `.claude/agents/adr-editor.md` is untracked and absent from `git diff HEAD` (`git status` shows `??`). If this patch is committed as-is, the new `subagent_type: "adr-editor"` references a missing agent definition.

The default capability assignment omits `adr-editor`, even though the same rule later routes `adr-editor` to Claude Code and the profile config defines it. This leaves the agent-facing routing guidance internally inconsistent for the new auto-invoked ADR editor capability.

The inserted `## 1.2 Track 前段階 + 3 フェーズ構成` section did not renumber the existing `## 1.2 Project Conventions` section. Later references such as `§1.2` are now ambiguous, so users can be sent to the wrong workflow section.

`/track:plan` forbids the main orchestrator from directly editing `spec.json` or any type catalogue during back-and-forth and says to re-invoke the original writer, but the new writer agents are explicitly read-only/advisory and state that the orchestrator performs the actual writes (`spec-designer` lines 28 and 89-90, `type-designer` lines 120-123). That leaves no actor authorized to persist Phase 1/2 fixes after a red signal, making the documented loop infeasible. Align the ownership model so either the orchestrator is allowed to apply writer output, or the specialist agents are actual file writers.

The quickstart command table still documents the old flow: `/track:plan` is described as planning only with domain type declaration left to `/track:design`, and as creating track artifacts after approval. The note at line 112 also shows `/track:plan -> approval -> /track:design -> /track:implement`, while the updated workflow later in the same file says `/track:plan` runs ADR pre-check plus Phase 0-3 including `/track:design`, and uses post-hoc artifact review rather than pre-approval. This user-facing guide now gives contradictory instructions for the primary planning path.

The default profile declares an `adr-editor` Claude capability and the docs route to `subagent_type: "adr-editor"`, but `.claude/agents/adr-editor.md` is not tracked by git (`git ls-files` omits it; it is only untracked in this worktree). A committed PR from this diff would leave the runnable profile pointing at a missing custom agent definition.

This says the main orchestrator re-invokes the writer and then applies the proposal to `knowledge/adr/`, `spec.json`, or catalogues. That contradicts the new ADR convention and `adr-editor` contract, which require `adr-editor` itself to edit ADR files and explicitly forbid main from directly editing `knowledge/adr/*.md`; following this text would violate the writer boundary.

The example says a Phase 2 design red signal directly invokes `adr-editor`, while `/track:plan`'s back-and-forth table routes Phase 2 red to `spec-designer` and only escalates to ADR editing if the mismatch propagates upstream. The two instructions give conflicting writer choices for the same failure mode.

`/track:design` still instructs agents to read `metadata.json` for task definitions, but the refactor makes Phase 0 metadata identity-only and moves tasks to Phase 3 `impl-plan.json`, which does not exist yet when Phase 2 design runs. This leaves the command asking for unavailable/stale data.

The orchestration rule's source-of-truth section still models a track around `metadata.json` plus rendered `spec.md`/`plan.md`, omitting the new authoritative artifacts (`spec.json`, `<layer>-types.json`, `impl-plan.json`, and `task-coverage.json`). Agents following this mandatory read list can skip the JSON SSoTs introduced by the refactor.

`/track:design` still requires a pre-write user review loop for generated type catalogues: it says to present the design before writing and iterate until the user accepts it. This contradicts the new ceremony-minimization policy and `/track:plan` gate policy, which restrict pre-approval to irreversible actions and require artifact generation to use post-hoc review of the actual files. Because `/track:plan` routes Phase 2 through `/track:design`, following this command preserves the obsolete pre-approval ceremony for a reversible track artifact.

The orchestration rule now defines `spec.json`, `<layer>-types.json`, `impl-plan.json`, and `task-coverage.json` as the Phase 1-3 SSoTs, but the mandatory “Read these first” source-of-truth list still only includes `metadata.json`, rendered `spec.md`/`plan.md`, and `verification.md`. Agents following this rule before planning or implementation can skip the new authoritative JSON artifacts and work from rendered views, undermining the refactor’s SSoT chain.

The default capability list omits `adr-editor`, even though this same refactor adds `capabilities.adr-editor` to `.harness/config/agent-profiles.json` and later sections describe `adr-editor` as a default Claude Code capability. This leaves the top-level user guide internally inconsistent about the available/default specialist capabilities for the new back-and-forth ADR workflow.

DEVELOPER_AI_WORKFLOW.md still describes `/track:design` as declaring only `domain-types.json`/domain types, while the refactored command and new Phase 2 flow now process every TDDD-enabled layer and produce `<layer>-types.json` catalogues. The same domain-only wording is repeated later in the standard flow, so the user-facing workflow remains inconsistent with the new multi-layer type-designer contract.

D1.1 says the ADR must not contain full trait/struct/method specifications, but D2.1 immediately embeds concrete Rust struct definitions for `SpecElementId`, `AdrAnchor`, `ConventionAnchor`, `ContentHash`, and the three ref types. That makes the ADR internally inconsistent about how much type detail it is allowed to spell out.

`SpecElementId` is only validated as a non-empty string in the task/verification text, but the ADR already fixes an ID naming convention (`IN-\d+`, `AC-\d+`, etc.). As written, the implementation can accept arbitrary ids, which weakens `SpecRef.anchor` and the new spec-element identity scheme.

The scope for T001/verification includes `track/workflow.md`, but the ADR rollout section only names `CLAUDE.md` and `DEVELOPER_AI_WORKFLOW.md`. That leaves one of the declared in-scope docs outside the ADR's own migration plan, so the file set is inconsistent across the documents.

D1.5 permits type catalogues to reference conventions, but the concrete ref schema only places `ConventionRef` on spec.json fields and D3.2 later says catalogues have no convention reference field at all. That gives implementers conflicting requirements: either catalogue entries need a `convention_refs` field and validator coverage, or the D1.5 permission must be removed.

The ADR lists `track/items/review-scope-prompt-injection-2026-04-18/review.json` as an observed-data reference, while the same ADR later forbids ADRs and new tracks from directly referencing track-internal artifacts to preserve lifecycle boundaries. The document should either move/summarize that evidence into a track-crossing artifact or define an explicit exception for historical observation data.

D2.3 says canonical-block detection implementation details are in Q15, but Q15 is about AdrAnchor/ConventionAnchor semantics and hash fields; Q14 is the canonical-block section. This broken reference points the T011/CLI implementation at the wrong design question.

D0.1 says any 🔴/🟡 SoT signal must trigger repair of the next upstream layer, but D3.1 and the /track:plan flow later define 🟡 as allowable and continue to the next phase. Implementers cannot satisfy both semantics: the orchestrator either loops on yellow or records a warning and proceeds.

The merge gate only requires task-coverage.json to cover all acceptance_criteria, while D1.4 defines coverage enforcement as covering both in_scope and acceptance_criteria. This weakens the final PR requirement relative to the task-coverage gate and leaves two conflicting definitions of what coverage is required for merge.

D4 says writers/capabilities must not span multiple phases, but the ownership table assigns the planner to both Phase 1 (/track:spec) and Phase 3 (/track:impl-plan). Since D1.4 also says the planner writes spec in Phase 1 and impl-plan/task-coverage in Phase 3, the writer-boundary rule cannot be enforced as written.

The canonical-block warning bullet points implementers to Q15 for details, but Q15 is about AdrAnchor/ConventionAnchor semantics and hash fields. The canonical-block unresolved decision is Q14, so this is a self-evidently wrong cross-reference.

D1.5 allows type catalog entries to reference conventions, but D3.2 says type catalogs have no convention reference field at all. D2.2 also lists `ConventionRef` use sites only for spec.json, so implementers cannot tell whether catalog `convention_refs` must exist and be verified or whether type-catalog-to-convention references are actually forbidden. This is a schema-level contradiction in the SoT reference policy.

The phase matrix still defines `Phase 3 完了` as `task-coverage.json` covering only all acceptance criteria and says merge is OK when SoT signals are blue. That conflicts with the stricter gate defined earlier: task coverage must cover both `in_scope` and `acceptance_criteria` with referential integrity, and final merge also requires every impl-plan task to be resolved. As written, this table can drive an implementation that permits a final merge with uncovered in-scope items or unresolved tasks.

This acceptance criterion is not satisfiable as written: it requires `informal_grounds[]` on type-catalogue entries to emit 🟡, but ADR D3.2 explicitly defers catalogue-side signal evaluation to a later ADR and only keeps the schema presence check in this scope. With only T003/T005 in scope, the track cannot meet this criterion unless you either narrow it to spec-side only or add a follow-on task for catalogue-side signal implementation.

The rendered graph still presents the approval/coverage model as live (`approved_at`, `effective_status`/`status`, `evaluate_coverage`, `task_refs`, `SpecStatus`, `CoverageResult`) even though the plan catalog deletes those concepts. That makes the plan-artifact set internally contradictory about the post-migration spec model; the graph and contract-map need to match the deleted-state intent or be explicitly marked legacy-only.

`TrackBlobReader` is declared with four methods in the usecase catalogue, but this contract map only renders two edges (`read_spec_document` and `read_track_metadata`). As written, the rendered map no longer describes the full port boundary and misstates the current contract surface.

The `ContentHash` entry describes the type as holding either a raw 32-byte buffer or a 64-character hex string. The ADR models `ContentHash` as a single normalized `[u8; 32]` value object, so this wording conflates constructor input formats with the stored domain representation.

`PlanArtifactRefsError` declares canonical-block suspicion as part of a public error enum, but the ADR/T011 design says canonical-block detection is warning-only. Implementing from this catalogue would turn an advisory warning into a fatal verifier error/CI failure, contradicting the planned gate semantics; canonical-block suspicion should be modeled as a warning finding instead of an error variant.

`SpecDocument` says every requirement, including `goal`, gains `id: SpecElementId` and the new ref fields, but the only modified requirement item contract (`SpecRequirement`) is documented as used only for `scope / constraints / acceptance_criteria`, and this catalogue declares no separate goal item type. That leaves the target representation for `goal[]` ambiguous and can let an implementation keep goals as bare strings while still following the `SpecRequirement` entry.

The `AdrRef` catalogue entry says ADR references are used in type catalogue entries, but the same plan’s SoT direction forbids type catalogue -> ADR direct references and `TypeCatalogueEntry` only adds `spec_refs` plus `informal_grounds`. Leaving this text in place gives implementers a contradictory schema target for catalogue entries.

The `ConventionRef` entry also claims use in type catalogue entries, while `TypeCatalogueEntry` explicitly says catalogue-level `convention_refs[]` is deferred to Q8 / a follow-up ADR. That contradicts the current-track scope, where catalogue entries should not gain convention reference fields yet.

ADR D1.4 defines identity-only metadata as retaining the track `status` field while moving only `tasks` and `plan` into `impl-plan.json`. This catalogue entry instead says TrackMetadata retains `status_override` and omits `status`; the matching TrackDocumentV2 entry repeats the same retained-field set. That gives implementers the wrong schema contract for metadata.json after task-derived status is removed, so the plan artifacts are internally inconsistent with the authoritative ADR.
